InnerSeed Garden · UX Development Manifesto · Week 2

UX Run-Through —
10–12 June 2026

Live session notes from the Week 2 UX run-through. Raw observations logged in full, with structured findings, screenshots, and a proposed structural solution grounded in UX research.

WeekWeek 2
Date10 June 2026
AuthorRuaraidh
Appinnerseed.garden
DeadlineFriday 13 June — focus call Jeroen
StatusIn progress
Session context. UX research brief active. Six areas to work through before the Friday 13 June focus call, where Ruaraidh presents the week’s findings to Jeroen for the first time on record.
01

Week 2 agenda

Six areas to cover before Friday 13 June:

  • Preset mode — as a regular user
  • Filter mode — intuitive and discoverable?
  • Saving sessions
  • Creating sessions
  • Favouriting sessions
  • Anything that feels unnatural or complex

Moving forward — keep it simple, stupid

Side note — to explore later

Analytics — how could this work within the app? Flag for future discussion.

Note — external UX input incoming

A UX professional outside of this project is reviewing the app informally at no charge. Look out for this input in Week 3 or 4 — it will be valuable to cross-reference against these findings.

02

Opening screen — five-question audit

The following five questions were applied to the opening screen. Raw answers are logged in full below each question, exactly as stated.

Fig. 1 — Full dashboard on load — nav collapsed to icons, “Build your breathwork” as dominant CTA, Presets and Builds sections below

Q1 — Clarity

Within 5 seconds, does the user know what this app is and what it does?

“No, in all honesty I don’t understand what Presets really means, and I have noticed that word confusing people when I show them or try explaining to them, they almost lose interest. And I’m fully convinced by the dashboard that I am here for breathwork, and that the build your breathwork thing is just floating there like it’s separate or I’m already meant to go for that? Where do I go? I’m lost. That’s me being honest.”

Fig. 2 — “Presets” section — three session cards visible, “See all” top right. The word “Presets” carries no meaning for new users in a breathwork context.

Q2 — Orientation

Does the user know where they are and what to do next? Is there a single obvious call to action, or are they faced with choices they’re not equipped to make yet?

“Only relevant to builder — so doesn’t mean much to me as a preset guy!?

Are these a snippet of many more of what’s on the menu? Why are they showing on the main screen? I am here to be a builder!

When I hover the cursor over the little house and the document logo etc on the left the menu pops out to tell me I can go do these 3 things, but I feel still like this has been built for people who know what they are here for — I am still learning and it feels a little less inviting for the untrained eye. My inclination is to hover in the middle and again, I am being told to take a moment and build my breathwork? I just wanna see what the app has to offer first?”

Fig. 3 — Nav expanded on hover, revealing Root / Rise / Return structure. Completely invisible to the user until they hover — no affordance on first load.

Fig. 4 — Nav in its default state — icon-only, no labels. A new user has no reason to hover here and no indication of what these icons mean.

Fig. 5 — “Builds” section below Presets — saved sessions displayed with no clear hierarchy or relationship to the Presets section above.

03

Structured findings from today’s session

UX Finding — Clarity

The word “Presets” is actively causing disengagement. Observed consistently when showing the app to others — interest drops at that word. It carries no meaning in a breathwork context. The dashboard succeeds in establishing that this is a breathwork product, but that clarity is immediately undermined by technical language the user doesn’t understand.

UX Finding — Orientation

The dashboard speaks two languages at once, and neither is welcoming to a new user. The session cards feel built for people who already know how to use the app. The “Build your breathwork” CTA feels like a task rather than an invitation. The left-hand nav only reveals on hover — a new user has no reason to look there and no indication it even exists.

The result: a user who wants to explore what the app offers has no clear path to do so. The screen assumes knowledge the user doesn’t have yet.

Design principle emerging — discovery first

A consistent theme across both findings: the app rewards users who already know what they want. What it needs is a first layer that helps users discover what the app offers before asking them to make a choice. The question it should answer on first load is not “what do you want to build?” but “here’s what this can do for you.”

04

Running findings log

RefFindingTypePriorityRecommendation
D-01“Presets” terminology confuses new users; observed causing disengagementUX / LanguageHighRename to “Sessions” throughout
D-02“Build your breathwork” CTA creates hierarchy confusion; new users don’t know what to do firstUX / IAHighRestructure hero — discovery first, builder secondary
D-03App detected as Dutch by browser on load for English usersTechnicalCriticalCorrect HTML lang attribute and language metadata
D-04Left-hand navigation invisible on first load — only appears on hoverUX / NavigationHighNav should be visible by default with permanent labels or affordance
D-05No discovery layer for first-time users — app assumes prior knowledgeUX / OnboardingHighFirst load should show what the app offers before asking the user to choose
D-06Tone leans “tech/gaming” rather than “wellness/calm”UX / Visual DesignMediumConsider warmer, softer treatment even within dark palette
D-07Hero banner image cuts off abruptly with a hard edgeUX / Visual DesignLow–MediumResize/crop properly or add gradient fade
D-08No single-click path to “experience something”UX / IAHighDashboard hero should lead to listening, not building
D-09Left nav labels describe process/stats, not experienceUX / NavigationHighAdd a clear “listen now” entry point
D-10Complexity doesn’t match simplicity of core taskUX / OverallHighRedesign with radical simplicity — “usable by a three year old”
D-11Mode confusion reproduced live — opening a Preset led into Builder modeUX / IAHighReinforces W1-01 — prioritise mode-switcher fix
D-12Session cards only clickable via tiny play iconUX / InteractionMediumMake whole card a tap target
D-13No way back from Preset player to dashboardUX / NavigationHighAdd back button or breadcrumb; reinforces W1-04
D-14Split InnerSeed logo is ambiguous as a home/nav buttonUX / NavigationMediumMake logo behave and look like a clear “home” link
D-15Player control panel doesn’t visually read as a breathwork sessionUX / Visual DesignMediumReinforces W1-05 — breathing visualiser would help
D-16Session metadata line (“CONTACT · Connect”) unclear, possible bugTechnicalHighDeveloper check — likely showing field key instead of value
D-17Transport bar icons (folder/settings/save) don’t fit Preset playback contextUX / IAHighBuilder-mode UI is bleeding into Preset mode — needs separate UI
D-18Favouriting works with clear feedback, but heart icon is small and “where do favourites go” is unansweredUX / Feedback LoopMediumLarger/higher-contrast icon; link to favourites location in confirmation
D-19“Load Session” modal too small — risks “illusion of completeness,” users miss content below the foldUX / LayoutMediumIncrease modal size; add scroll affordance (partial card cut-off)
D-20Load Session modal shows same tabs regardless of mode; “My Sessions” shows built sessions, not favourites; no dedicated Favourites tabUX / IAHighContext-aware tabs, or add a Favourites tab
D-21Major — App has three disconnected navigation systems (sidebar, Session Settings modal, Load Session modal) that don’t map onto each other or adapt to active modeUX / IA — StructuralCritical“Threefold consistency” pattern — see Section 09
05

Opening screen audit — Q3, Q4, Q5

Fig. 6 — Hero banner image ends on a hard horizontal edge, with flat black filling the remaining space below

Q3 — Trust & tone

The dashboard feels sleek and intelligent — but in a “tech/gaming” sense rather than a calm, wellness sense. Dark mode reads as almost game-like. The “take a moment to ground yourself” text floating in the hero needs attention, and the banner image below cuts off abruptly — not pleasant on the eye.

Finding D-06 — Tone leans “tech/gaming” rather than “wellness/calm”

For an app whose core promise is calm and presence, this is a tonal mismatch worth flagging. Worth considering: is dark mode right for this product, or does it need warmer, softer treatment — even within a dark palette (warmer greys, organic shapes, softer typography)?

Finding D-07 — Hero banner image cuts off awkwardly

The landscape image ends on a hard horizontal line with flat black below it — looks unfinished rather than intentional. Either the image needs to be sized/cropped properly to fill its container, or the transition needs a gradient fade so it blends rather than cuts.

Q4 — Friction

The first thing drawing attention is “Build your breathwork” — it reads as the only option presented, with no indication it’s one of several. “Presets” doesn’t communicate “guided breathworks” the way a more familiar term would. The left-hand nav (Preparation, Session Builder, The Green House) suggests the only thing to do here is build sessions. There’s no clear, single “you can do this right now if you click it” moment.

Finding D-08 — No single-click path to “experience something”

Even with sessions visible on the dashboard, the dominant CTA (“Build your breathwork”) draws focus toward construction, not consumption. A new user wanting to just try the app has no obvious “press play and feel it” moment within the first interaction.

Finding D-09 — Left nav reinforces “builder-only” impression

“Preparation,” “Session Builder,” “The Green House” — all three labels describe process and stats, not experience. Nothing says “listen to something now.” It reads as a workspace for builders, not a front door for everyone.

Q5 — Return user consideration

As a returning user, the layout still needs improvement and clearer structure. For a homepage this simple, it should be far more intuitive — almost usable by a child. Worth approaching the design through that lens: the simpler the core task, the simpler the interface needs to be.

Finding D-10 — Complexity doesn’t match simplicity of purpose

The core action (start a breathwork session) is simple, but the interface requires interpretation regardless of experience level. A returning user benefits from familiarity but still has to work around the same structural issues as a first-timer.

Five-question audit: complete

The full opening screen audit (Q1–Q5) is now complete — ten findings total (D-01 to D-10), grounded in the “discovery first” principle established earlier in the week.

06

Step 1 — Preset mode: live testing (Ethereal Voyager)

Testing began with the goal of opening a Preset session. The first attempt instead landed in Session Builder — reproducing the Week 1 mode-discoverability finding (W1-01) live, under real conditions.

Fig. 7 — Landed in “Build your session” (Session Builder) instead of a Preset — Ambient Music, Guided Breaths, Guided Voices, 20:00 timer

Fig. 8 — The Session Settings modal, revealed via the cog — Preset / Builder (selected) / Creator (Facilitator)

Finding D-11 — Mode confusion is real, not theoretical

Attempting to open a Preset led instead into Session Builder, with no indication of which mode was active until the settings cog was manually opened. This directly reproduces W1-01 under live conditions — strong evidence for prioritising the mode-switcher fix.

Returning to the dashboard, “Ethereal Voyager” was opened correctly via its small play icon.

Finding D-12 — Session cards only clickable via tiny play icon

Clicking the Ethereal Voyager card itself did nothing — only the small play button registered. The whole card should be a tap target, especially on touch devices.

Finding D-13 — No way back from a Preset player to the dashboard

Once inside a session, there’s no back button, no breadcrumb, and the side nav options (Preparation, Session Builder, The Green House) don’t map to “return to dashboard.” Directly related to W1-04, but identified before a session even finishes, not just after.

Finding D-14 — The InnerSeed logo is split and ambiguous as a nav element

The logo reads as two separate elements (“Inner” / “Seed”), with no clear indication it acts as a “home” button. Logos are a near-universal “return home” convention; if this one doesn’t work that way, it should, and it should look unmistakably clickable.

Fig. 9 — Ethereal Voyager player — artwork panel with title, metadata and favourite icon; control panel below with timer and transport icons

Finding D-15 — The control panel doesn’t read as a “session player”

The layout is simple but feels bare rather than calm. Without having clicked through from Ethereal Voyager, a user wouldn’t necessarily know they were about to listen to a breathwork session — no breathing-related cue, language, or visual element signals this. Connects to W1-05 — a breathing visualiser would give this panel a calm, on-brand identity.

Fig. 10 — Session metadata line: “21 min · CONTACT · Connect · ♂ Jeroen”

Finding D-16 — Session metadata line is unclear, possible bug

“CONTACT” and “Connect” appearing side-by-side don’t form coherent information. Possibilities: “CONTACT” may be displaying a field key instead of its value (a common labelling bug); “Connect” may be the actual session category; “♂ Jeroen” presumably credits the creator, though the male gender symbol is an odd authorship indicator. Flagged for developer check — likely a bug rather than a design issue.

Fig. 11 — Transport bar: folder, settings (cog), play, stop, save

Finding D-17 — Transport bar icons don’t fit a Preset playback context

On a Preset playback screen, a user expects controls related to listening. Instead: the folder icon implies browsing/organising files; the settings cog implies editing the session (but this is a ready-made preset); the save icon implies saving a new creation. This strongly suggests the Preset player is reusing the Session Builder’s transport bar UI without adapting it for playback context — the same pattern seen in D-11 and D-15.

07

Favouriting & the “Load Session” modal

UX research framework applied

From this point, findings are evaluated against established usability research. Nielsen’s heuristics emphasise that keeping users informed about what’s happening, with appropriate feedback, prevents confusion and frustration — when a user takes an action, they expect the product to react (Nielsen, 1994).

Fig. 12 — Clicking the heart icon produces an immediate “Added to favorites” confirmation toast

Finding D-18 — Favouriting works, but creates an “orphaned action”

Clicking the heart icon triggers a clear, immediate confirmation — this satisfies Nielsen’s visibility of system status heuristic well. However, two issues remain: the heart icon itself is small and low-contrast, easy to miss on first glance; and where favourited items go is unclear — no “View favorites” option in the toast, and no obvious “Favorites” section on the dashboard.

Good micro-interactions exist, but they’re not yet woven into a coherent navigational system. The pieces work individually but don’t yet form a map a user can hold in their head.

Fig. 13 — The folder icon opens a “Load Session” modal — My Sessions / Presets / Shared with me. Ethereal Voyager’s heart is now filled red, confirming the favourite persisted

Finding D-19 — “Load Session” modal too small for its purpose

The modal’s small fixed window shows roughly three items before requiring scroll. Nielsen Norman Group research shows users allocate only around 20% of their attention to content below the fold, and often decide whether to continue based on what’s immediately visible (NN/G, 2018). Bruce Tognazzini’s “illusion of completeness” (1998) describes how a screen that looks complete causes users to assume they’ve seen everything and move on.

In a small modal like this, a user with several saved or favourited sessions may see three, assume that’s the full list, and never scroll. Recommendation: increase the modal’s height/width to show more items, and/or add a clear scroll affordance (a partial fourth card cut off at the bottom).

Fig. 14 — “My Sessions” tab loaded — shows two self-built sessions, not favourited presets

Finding D-20 — The “Load Session” modal mixes content types without mode-awareness

The same three tabs (My Sessions / Presets / Shared with me) appear regardless of whether the user is in Preset or Builder mode. Clicking “My Sessions” loaded two self-built sessions — not favourites as might be expected — leaving the user unsure whether they were still in Preset mode or had moved into Builder mode.

For a user in Preset mode, “My Sessions” (built sessions) may be irrelevant. Connects directly to D-11 and D-17 — the recurring pattern of Builder-mode and Preset-mode interfaces not being meaningfully separated.

Two design directions: (1) context-aware tabs — the modal shows different tabs depending on current mode; or (2) a dedicated “Favourites” tab, regardless of mode, resolving the open question from D-18.

08

Session Settings modal — structural finding

Fig. 15 — Session Settings modal, reached via the cog while inside a Preset session

Fig. 16 — Full view — left sidebar (Preparation, Session Builder, The Green House) alongside the Preset player, showing the disconnect between the two navigation systems

Finding D-21 — The app lacks a consistent structural pattern across modes (Major)

Three separate, disconnected navigation systems currently coexist: the left sidebar (Preparation / Session Builder / The Green House), the Session Settings modal (Preset / Builder / Creator), and the Load Session modal (My Sessions / Presets / Shared with me). None of these map onto each other, and none change based on which mode is active.

The settings cog, encountered while inside a Preset session, does not offer Preset-specific settings — instead it opens the global mode switcher, pulling the user out of their current context entirely. This breaks the user’s mental model: an action taken inside an experience pulls them out of it.

This single finding connects and explains D-11, D-17, D-18 and D-20 — five findings, one root cause.

09

Proposed solution — “threefold consistency”

A single structural pattern that resolves D-11, D-17, D-18, D-20 and D-21 together: each of the three modes (Preset, Builder, Creator) offers the same three-part structure, adapted to its purpose.

Tab 1Tab 2 (primary)Tab 3
Preset modeMy FavouritesSessions (browse)Shared with me
Builder modeMy SessionsBuild a SessionShared with me
Creator modeMy RecordingsCreateShared with me
Preset
Builder
Creator
My favourites
Saved sessions
Sessions
Browse all presets
Shared with me
From others
Preset
Builder
Creator
My sessions
Sessions I’ve built
Build a session
Create from scratch
Shared with me
From others
Preset
Builder
Creator
My recordings
Voice recordings
Create
Add your voice
Shared with me
From others

Fig. 17 — Threefold consistency mockup — the same three-tab structure repeated across all three modes, with mode-appropriate content. The active tab in each panel shows “you are here”

Why this matters

This would unify the left sidebar, the Session Settings modal, and the Load Session modal into one predictable pattern — wherever the user is, the structure feels familiar, just filled with mode-appropriate content. It is the single highest-impact recommendation arising from Week 2: rather than five separate fixes, one structural principle resolves all of them.

10

Sources & references

UX research grounding

Nielsen, J. (1994) 10 Usability Heuristics for User Interface Design, Nielsen Norman Group.

Nielsen Norman Group (2018) Scrolling and Attention.

Tognazzini, B. (1998) The “Illusion of Completeness.”
